top_categories.workout_context: Race-pace efforts that mimic aQuelle Tour Durban's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before April.
54ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
When someone attacks on paved coastal highway along the Indian Ocean, you have 10 seconds to respond or you're off the back. This builds that response.
aQuelle Tour Durban's paved coastal highway along the Indian Ocean forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 68 miles.
68 miles on paved coastal highway along the Indian Ocean burns energy faster than the distance suggests. Train the fade resistance now.
At aQuelle Tour Durban, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of aQuelle Tour Durban. Train it until it feels boring.
Varied gradients on paved coastal highway along the Indian Ocean demand climbing versatility. Sit the shallow stuff, stand the steep stuff, switch without thinking.
Short, max efforts are unavoidable. Punchy climbs, gap closures, attacks on paved coastal highway along the Indian Ocean. If you haven't trained them, you'll crack.
How long can you hold 105% FTP before you blow? At aQuelle Tour Durban, that number decides whether you bridge or get dropped.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 3,727ft of climbing on paved coastal highway along the Indian Ocean does to your power file on race day.
Double-threshold training builds sustained power with less fatigue. More FTP per hour of training.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 40–60g carbs/hour for aQuelle Tour Durban’s 68 miles. Front-load calories in the first half. One bottle per hour minimum, more in heat.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Race Simulation, TT Threshold, and VO2max to prepare you for 68 miles of paved coastal highway along the indian ocean in Durban, KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa.
